1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]

1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree

1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[

22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	
25	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
26	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
27	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
28	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
29	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
30	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
31	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
32	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
33	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
34	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
35	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
36	
37	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
38	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
39	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
40	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
41	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
42	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
43	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
44	\[
45	  \begin{array}{ccc}
46	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
47	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
48	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
49	  \end{array}
50	\]
51	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
52	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
53	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
54	
55	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
56	
57	\begin{definition}
58	
59	\leanok
60	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
61	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
63	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
64	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
65	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
66	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
67	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
68	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
69	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
70	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
71	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
72	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
73	  % the unit ideal, see
74	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
75	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
76	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
77	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
78	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
79	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
80	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
81	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
82	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
83	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
84	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
85	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
86	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
87	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
88	  \[
89	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
90	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
91	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
92	  \]
93	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
94	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
95	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
96	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
97	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
98	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
99	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
100	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
101	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
102	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
103	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
104	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
105	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
106	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
107	\end{definition}
108	
109	\begin{definition}
110	
111	\leanok
112	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
113	  \label{def:cech_complex}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
115	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
116	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
117	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
118	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
119	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
120	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
121	  % is identified with the complex
122	  % $$
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % $$
128	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
129	  % \begin{equation}
130	  % \label{equation-extended}
131	  % 0 \to
132	  % M \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
134	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
135	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
136	  % \ldots
137	  % \end{equation}
138	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
139	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
140	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
141	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
142	  vanishing).}
143	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
144	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
145	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
146	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
147	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
148	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
149	  in degree \(p\) it is
150	  \[
151	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
152	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
153	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
154	  \]
155	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
156	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
157	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
158	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
159	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
160	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
161	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
162	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
163	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
164	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
165	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
166	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
167	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
168	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
169	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
170	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
171	\end{definition}

173	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
174	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
175	
176	It is convenient to factor the construction of
177	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
178	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
179	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
180	
181	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
182	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
183	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
184	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
185	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
186	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
187	\[
188	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
189	    \;=\;
190	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
191	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
192	\]
193	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
194	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
195	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
196	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
197	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
198	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
199	spaces over which the construction is indexed.

202	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
203	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
204	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
205	\[
206	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
207	  \qquad
208	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
209	\]
210	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
211	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
212	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
213	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
214	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
215	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
216	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
217	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
218	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
219	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
220	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
221	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
222	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
223	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
224	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
225	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
226	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
227	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
228	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
229	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
230	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
231	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
232	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
233	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
234	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
235	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
236	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
237	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
238	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
239	
240	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
241	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
242	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
243	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
244	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
245	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
246	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
247	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
248	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
249	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
250	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
251	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
252	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
253	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
254	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
255	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
256	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
257	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
258	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
259	
260	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
261	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
262	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
263	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
264	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
265	
266	\begin{definition}\leanok
267	[Arrow of an affine cover]
268	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
270	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
271	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
272	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
273	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
274	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
275	  apply.
276	\end{definition}
277	
278	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
279	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
281	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
282	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
283	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
284	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
285	  \[
286	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
287	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
288	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
289	  \]
290	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
291	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
292	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\begin{definition}\leanok
296	[Push--pull object map]
297	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
299	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
300	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
301	  \[
302	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
303	  \]
304	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
305	\end{definition}
306	
307	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
338	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
340	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
341	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
342	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
343	\end{lemma}
344	
345	\begin{proof}
346	
347	\leanok
348	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
349	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
350	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
351	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
352	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\begin{lemma}
356	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
357	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
359	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
361	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
362	  \[
363	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
364	  \]
365	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
366	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
367	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
368	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
369	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
370	\end{lemma}
371	
372	\begin{proof}
373	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
374	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
375	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
376	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
377	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
378	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
379	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
380	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
381	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
382	\end{proof}
383	
384	\begin{lemma}
385	
386	\leanok
387	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
388	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
390	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
391	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
392	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
393	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
394	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
395	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
396	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
397	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
398	  \[
399	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
400	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
401	    \;=\;
402	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
403	  \]
404	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
405	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
406	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
407	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
408	\end{lemma}
409	
410	\begin{proof}
411	
412	\leanok
413	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
414	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
415	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
416	\end{proof}

461	  \[
462	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
463	  \]
464	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of

466	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
467	  laws established above: the identity law
468	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
469	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
470	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.

495	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
496	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
497	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
498	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
499	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
500	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
501	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is

506	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
507	
508	\begin{definition}
509	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
510	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
512	  \mathlibok
513	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
514	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
515	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
516	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
517	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
518	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
519	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
520	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
521	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
522	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
523	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
524	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
525	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
526	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
527	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
528	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
529	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
530	\end{definition}
531	
532	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
533	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
534	
535	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
536	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
537	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
538	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
539	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
540	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
541	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
542	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
543	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
544	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
545	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
546	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
547	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
548	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
549	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
550	
551	\begin{definition}
552	
553	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
554	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
556	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
563	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
564	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
565	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
566	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
567	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
568	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
569	  % as an $R_f$-module.
570	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
571	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
572	  % are the maps
573	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
574	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
575	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
576	  items (4)--(5).}
577	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
578	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
579	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
580	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
581	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
582	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
583	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
584	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
585	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
586	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
587	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
588	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
589	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
590	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
591	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
592	
593	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
594	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
595	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
596	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
597	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
598	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
599	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
600	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
601	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
602	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
603	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
604	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
605	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
606	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
607	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
608	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
609	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
610	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
611	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
612	
613	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
614	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
615	  intersection identity
616	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
617	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
618	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
619	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
620	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
621	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
622	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
623	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
624	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
625	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
626	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
627	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
628	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
629	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
630	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
631	
632	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
633	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
634	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
635	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
636	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
637	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
638	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
639	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
640	  restriction map through these comparisons.
641	\end{definition}

643	\begin{lemma}
644	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
645	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
647	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
648	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
649	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
650	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
651	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
652	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
653	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
654	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
655	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
656	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
657	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
658	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
659	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
660	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
661	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
662	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
663	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
664	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
665	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
666	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
667	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
668	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
669	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
670	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
671	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
672	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
673	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
674	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
675	  % L58--67).
676	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
677	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
678	  % is identified with the complex
679	  % $$
680	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
681	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
682	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
683	  % \ldots
684	  % $$"
685	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
686	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
687	  complex of localisations).}
688	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
689	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
690	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
691	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
692	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
693	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
694	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
695	  \[
696	    D^\bullet : \quad
697	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
698	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
699	    \cdots,
700	    \qquad
701	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
702	  \]
703	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
704	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
705	  \[
706	    1 \leq p
707	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
708	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
709	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
710	  \]
711	  which is the form the affine vanishing
712	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
713	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
714	  homomorphisms,
715	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
716	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
717	
718	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
719	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
720	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
721	  product}
722	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
723	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
724	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
725	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
726	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
727	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
728	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
729	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
730	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
731	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
732	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
734	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
735	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
736	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
737	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
738	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
739	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
740	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
742	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
743	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
744	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
745	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
746	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
747	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
748	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the

750	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
751	\end{lemma}
752	
753	\begin{proof}
754	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
755	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
756	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
757	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
758	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
759	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
760	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
761	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
762	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
763	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
764	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
765	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
766	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
767	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
768	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
769	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
770	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
771	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
772	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
773	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
774	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
775	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
776	  element-level product equivalence
777	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
778	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
779	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
780	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
781	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
782	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
783	
784	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
785	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
786	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
787	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
788	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
789	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
790	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
791	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
792	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
793	  being proved.
794	\end{proof}
795	
796	\begin{lemma}
797	
798	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
799	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
801	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
802	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
803	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
804	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
805	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
806	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
807	  an equivalence of underlying types
808	  \[

903	  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
904	  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component
905	  projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_i\) and post-composes with the presheaf
906	  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\). Reading the
907	  \(\sigma\)-coordinate through the product equivalence (the coordinate-projection

909	  past the categorical sum: applying the sum of \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-morphisms to an element

1221	  (\(\mathcal{F} : (\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\)). Let

1224	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in \iota} D(s_i)\) be the associated standard
1225	  affine cover of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}; the index set and the
1226	  affine pieces \(D(s_i)\) with sections \(R_{s_i}\) are read off directly from the
1227	  spanning family. The statement is \emph{intrinsic} to \(\operatorname{Spec} R\):
1228	  there is no relative morphism \(f\) and no pushforward in the conclusion. Then the
1229	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1230	  \mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- the cochain
1231	  complex of abelian groups whose degree-\(p\) term is
1232	  \(\prod_{\sigma}\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) --- has vanishing cohomology in all

1235	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,

1282	  \paragraph{Reduction to the localised complex.}
1283	  By the section identification of Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, each
1284	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap D(s_{\sigma(p)})\)
1285	  is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
1286	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)}\), and its sections are the away localisation
1287	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)) = M_{s_\sigma}\); under these identifications each face
1288	  restriction is the canonical localisation map. Hence
1289	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} identifies the section {\v C}ech complex
1290	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1291	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} with the concrete complex of localised
1292	  \(R\)-modules
1293	  \[
1294	    \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \;\to\;
1295	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}} \;\to\;
1296	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1} s_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
1297	  \]
1298	  and reduces the vanishing
1299	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1300	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\) for \(p > 0\) to positive-degree
1301	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying localisation maps. Equivalently,
1302	  the extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
1303	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is
1304	  exact in positive degrees. The active target is the tilde case
1305	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), where the section identification of
1306	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} is gap-free and the coface match is
1307	  the abstract unfold of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} composed with the
1308	  tilde bridge of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}; a general quasi-coherent
1309	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case by \(\mathcal{F} \cong
1310	  \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the separately-deferred
1311	  globalisation gap.

1313	  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}
1314	  Exactness of each positive-degree node is established from the spanning family
1315	  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
1316	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)
1317	  reduces \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p-1}, d^{p})\) to exactness after
1318	  localising at each spanning element \(r\) (the away localisation
1319	  \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)), checking exactness one localisation at a time. In the
1320	  localisation at \(s_r\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes \(s_r\)
1321	  invertible, so the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport \(c\)
1322	  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
1323	  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
1324	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
1325	  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
1326	  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
1327	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
1328	  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
1329	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
1330	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
1331	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
1332	  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
1333	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
1334	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
1335	  abstract section complex.
1336	
1337	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1338	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1339	  extra-degeneracy packaging does not apply here (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map
1340	  is only a section-level homotopy after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra
1341	  degeneracy), so the concrete
1342	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1343	  is used instead.
1344	\end{proof}

1350	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1351	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1352	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1353	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1354	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1355	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1356	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1357	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1358	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1359	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1360	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1361	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1362	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1363	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1364	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1365	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1366	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1367	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1368	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1369	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1393	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1394	  % $$
1395	  % \ldots
1396	  % \to
1397	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}

1402	  % \to 0 \to \ldots

1413	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1414	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1415	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1416	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1417	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1418	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1419	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1420	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1421	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1422	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1423	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1424	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1425	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1426	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1427	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1429	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1430	  \[
1431	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1432	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1433	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1434	  \]
1435	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1436	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1437	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1438	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1439	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1440	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1441	  \[
1442	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1443	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1444	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1445	  \]
1446	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1447	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1448	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1449	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1450	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1451	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1452	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1453	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1454	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1455	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1456	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1457	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1458	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1459	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1460	
1461	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1462	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1463	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1464	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1465	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1466	
1467	  The parallel \texttt{...Fam} declarations pinned above are the cover-agnostic
1468	  raw-finite-family mirror of this construction — indexed by an arbitrary finite
1469	  family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens carrying \emph{no} covering hypothesis — and
1470	  feed the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma
1471	  (\(\operatorname{injective\_cech\_acyclicFam}\)).
1472	\end{definition}

1506	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1507	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1508	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1509	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1510	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1511	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1512	  \[
1513	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1514	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1515	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1516	  \]
1517	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1518	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1519	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1520	  \[
1521	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1522	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1523	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1524	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1525	  \]
1526	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1527	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1528	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1529	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1530	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1531	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1532	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1533	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the

1614	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,

1618	    \bigr)

1622	    \;\cong\;

1624	  \]

1626	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,

1628	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =

7476	\begin{lemma}
7477	[A contracting homotopy of the identity makes homology vanish]
7478	  \label{lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7480	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a preadditive category and \(D^\bullet\) a cochain complex in
7481	  \(\mathcal{A}\). If there is a homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet} \simeq 0\) between the
7482	  identity endomorphism of \(D^\bullet\) and the zero endomorphism, then for every degree
7483	  \(p\) the homology object \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) is a zero object.
7484	\end{lemma}
7485	\begin{proof}
7486	  The map induced on homology is a homotopy invariant: homotopic chain maps induce equal
7487	  maps on each homology object. Applying this to the homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}
7488	  \simeq 0\), the map induced by \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}\) on \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) equals the
7489	  map induced by \(0\). The former is \(\mathrm{id}_{H^p(D^\bullet)}\) (homology is a
7490	  functor) and the latter is the zero endomorphism of \(H^p(D^\bullet)\); hence
7491	  \(\mathrm{id}_{H^p(D^\bullet)} = 0\). An object whose identity morphism equals its zero
7492	  endomorphism is a zero object, so \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) is zero.
7493	\end{proof}
7494	
7495	\begin{lemma}
7496	[The degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech backbone is the coproduct of intersection opens]
7497	  \label{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}
7498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechBackbone_left_sigma}
7499	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7500	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an open cover with cover arrow
7501	  \(q : \coprod_{i \in I} U_i \to X\), and let \(Y_p\) denote the degree-\(p\) object of the
7502	  {\v C}ech nerve \(\operatorname{coverCechNerveOver}\mathcal{U}\) of
7503	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} --- the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of \(q\) over
7504	  \(X\). Then \(Y_p\) is canonically the coproduct
7505	  \[
7506	    Y_p \;\cong\; \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I} U_\sigma,
7507	    \qquad
7508	    U_\sigma \;=\; \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \;=\;
7509	      \bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)},
7510	  \]
7511	  indexed by the multi-indices \(\sigma\), and the structure map \(q_p : Y_p \to X\) is
7512	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\), where
7513	  \(j_\sigma : U_\sigma \hookrightarrow X\) is the open immersion of the intersection open
7514	  \(U_\sigma\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}. In words: the fibre power
7515	  of the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\) is the intersection open, and the disjoint
7516	  union over \(\sigma\) assembles the {\v C}ech backbone.
7517	\end{lemma}
7518	\begin{proof}
7519	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7520	  The coproduct \(\coprod_i U_i\) has, in degree \(p\), the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power over
7521	  \(X\); fibre products distribute over coproducts, so \(\bigl(\coprod_i U_i\bigr)^{\times_X
7522	  (p+1)} \cong \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I} U_{\sigma(0)} \times_X \cdots
7523	  \times_X U_{\sigma(p)}\). Each factor \(U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X\) is an open
7524	  immersion, and the fibre product of open immersions over \(X\) is the intersection of
7525	  their images, so \(U_{\sigma(0)} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{\sigma(p)} \cong \bigcap_k
7526	  U_{\sigma(k)} = U_\sigma\). Under this identification the structure map of the
7527	  \(\sigma\)-component is the open immersion \(j_\sigma\), and the universal map out of the
7528	  coproduct is \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\).
7529	\end{proof}

7531	\begin{lemma}
7532	[Push--pull over the {\v C}ech backbone is the product over intersection opens]
7533	  \label{lem:pushPull_sigma_iso}
7534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_sigma_iso}
7535	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma, lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib,
7536	    lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib}
7537	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}, the push--pull object on
7538	  the degree-\(p\) backbone decomposes as a product over the multi-indices:
7539	  \[
7540	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_p
7541	      \;\cong\;
7542	    \prod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
7543	      \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk} j_\sigma)
7544	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules},
7545	  \]
7546	  equivalently \((q_p)_*\,(q_p)^*\mathcal{F} \cong \prod_\sigma (j_\sigma)_*\,
7547	  (j_\sigma)^*\mathcal{F}\).
7548	\end{lemma}
7549	\begin{proof}
7550	  \uses{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma, lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib,
7551	    lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib}
7552	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma} the backbone \(Y_p\) is the coproduct
7553	  \(\coprod_\sigma U_\sigma\) with structure map \(q_p = \operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma
7554	  \mapsto j_\sigma)\). A sheaf of modules on a coproduct scheme is the product of its
7555	  restrictions to the components: although the images \(U_\sigma \subseteq X\) overlap, the
7556	  components are \emph{disjoint inside the coproduct space} \(\coprod_\sigma U_\sigma\), so
7557	  the sheaf condition over the disjoint cover by the components expresses the sections over
7558	  any open as the product of the sections over the component traces. Pulling \(\mathcal{F}\)
7559	  back along \(q_p\) and pushing forward therefore yields, componentwise, the push--pull of
7560	  \(\mathcal{F}\) along each \(j_\sigma\).
7561	
7562	  Concretely, build the module-linear comparison map \(\operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,
7563	  Y_p \to \prod_\sigma \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk}
7564	  j_\sigma)\) from the projections induced by the coproduct inclusions \(U_\sigma
7565	  \hookrightarrow Y_p\). To check it is an isomorphism it suffices, since the forgetful
7566	  functor \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf}\) is faithful, reflects isomorphisms,
7567	  and preserves limits, to verify the statement on the underlying presheaf of abelian
7568	  groups. There the comparison is the indexed disjoint-union sheaf decomposition: the binary
7569	  case is \(\operatorname{coprodPresheafObjIso}\) of
7570	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib} (sections over a binary coproduct scheme are
7571	  the product of the sections over the two preimages) and, for an abstract sheaf on a space,
7572	  \(\operatorname{isProductOfDisjoint}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib} (for
7573	  \(U \sqcap V = \bot\), \(F(U \sqcup V) \cong F(U) \times F(V)\)); iterating the binary
7574	  decomposition over the finite index set \(\{\sigma\}\) gives the indexed product.
7575	  Transporting the resulting presheaf isomorphism back through
7576	  \(\operatorname{toPresheaf}\) shows the module-linear comparison is an isomorphism.
7577	\end{proof}

7584	    lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}

7586	  open \(U_\sigma = \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma\), and let
7587	  \(V \subseteq X\) be an open. Then there is a natural isomorphism of section groups
7588	  \[

7590	      j_\sigma)\bigr)
7591	      \;\cong\;
7592	    \Gamma\bigl(U_\sigma \cap V,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr).
7593	  \]

7596	  \uses{lem:pushforward_obj_obj_mathlib, lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
7597	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
7598	  This follows from three standard identifications. First, the leg is
7599	  \((j_\sigma)_*\,(j_\sigma)^*\mathcal{F}\); pushforward sections are computed by preimage,
7600	  \(\Gamma\bigl(V, (j_\sigma)_* N\bigr) = \Gamma\bigl(j_\sigma^{-1}(V), N\bigr)\)
7601	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_obj_obj_mathlib}, an equality), applied with \(N =
7602	  (j_\sigma)^*\mathcal{F}\). Second, pullback along the open immersion \(j_\sigma\) is the
7603	  restriction functor: \((j_\sigma)^*\mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}.\operatorname{restrict}
7604	  j_\sigma\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}. Third, the sections of a
7605	  restricted module over \(j_\sigma^{-1}(V)\) are the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the
7606	  image open (Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}, an equality): \(\Gamma\bigl(j_\sigma^{-1}
7607	  (V), \mathcal{F}.\operatorname{restrict} j_\sigma\bigr) = \Gamma\bigl(j_\sigma\bigl(
7608	  j_\sigma^{-1}(V)\bigr), \mathcal{F}\bigr) = \Gamma\bigl(U_\sigma \cap V, \mathcal{F}\bigr)\),

7611	  three gives the stated isomorphism.

7614	\begin{lemma}
7615	[Degreewise section identification of the {\v C}ech backbone]
7616	  \label{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso}

7618	  \uses{lem:pushPull_sigma_iso, lem:pushPull_leg_sections,
7619	    lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}

7621	  \[
7622	    \Gamma\bigl(V,\, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_p\bigr)
7623	      \;\cong\;

7630	    lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}

7632	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_sigma_iso}), \(\operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_p \cong
7633	  \prod_\sigma \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk} j_\sigma)\),
7634	  with the fact that the section (evaluation-at-\(V\)) functor on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)

7636	  \prod_\sigma N_\sigma) \cong \prod_\sigma \Gamma(V, N_\sigma)\). This reduces the
7637	  left-hand side to \(\prod_\sigma \Gamma\bigl(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,

7642	
7643	\begin{lemma}
7644	[The evaluated augmented {\v C}ech complex is the concrete section {\v C}ech complex]
7645	  \label{lem:cechSection_complex_iso}
7646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechSection_complex_iso}
7647	  \uses{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
7648	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, def:cech_augmented_complex}
7649	  Fix an open \(V \subseteq X\). The augmented {\v C}ech complex of
7650	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmented_complex} evaluated at \(V\) (through
7651	  \(\operatorname{toPresheaf}\)) --- call it \(D^\bullet\), the cochain complex of abelian
7652	  groups with \(D^p = \Gamma(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_p)\) --- is
7653	  isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to the concrete section {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\)
7654	  over the family \(\bigl(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\cdot \cap V\bigr)\): the
7655	  complex with \(D'^p = \prod_\sigma \Gamma(U_\sigma \cap V, \mathcal{F})\) and the
7656	  alternating coface restriction differential. The isomorphism is the degreewise map of

7661	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
7662	  In each degree \(p\) the object isomorphism \(D^p \cong D'^p\) is
7663	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso}. It remains to check the degreewise isomorphisms
7664	  commute with the differentials. The differential of \(D'^\bullet\) is, read through the
7665	  product equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\)
7666	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), the alternating sum of presheaf face
7667	  restrictions \(\sum_i (-1)^i\, \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) computed in
7668	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}. The differential of \(D^\bullet\) is the

7672	  agree. Reusing the alternating-sum bookkeeping of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
7673	  --- rather than rebuilding it --- matches the differentials and yields the isomorphism of

7677	\begin{lemma}
7678	[The section {\v C}ech complex inside a cover member is contractible]
7679	  \label{lem:cechSection_contractible}
7680	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechSection_contractible}
7681	  \uses{lem:cechSection_complex_iso, lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_engine_complex,
7682	    def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7683	  Suppose \(V \le \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) for some fixed index
7684	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), i.e.\ \(V\) lies inside one cover member. Then the concrete section
7685	  {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso} over the family
7686	  \(U'_j = \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,j \cap V\) admits a contracting homotopy
7687	  \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet} \simeq 0\).
7688	\end{lemma}
7689	\begin{proof}
7690	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_engine_complex}
7691	  Because \(V \le U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}\), the restricted family \(U'_j = U_j \cap V\) has a
7692	  \emph{maximum}: \(U'_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} = U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \cap V = V\), and every
7693	  \(U'_j \le V\). Consequently, for each multi-index \(\sigma\),
7694	  \(U'_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}\,\sigma} = U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \cap U_\sigma \cap V = U_\sigma \cap
7695	  V = U'_\sigma\): prepending the index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) does not change the open, so the
7696	  prepend map is the \emph{identity} on each coefficient \(\Gamma(U'_\sigma, \mathcal{F})\)
7697	  (the membership criterion \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\) of
7698	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex}). The dependent-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech
7699	  engine --- \(\operatorname{depDiff}\), \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\),
7700	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy\_spec}\), \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of
7701	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} --- then supplies the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)
7702	  contracting homotopy \(h\) with \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\), since its unit,
7703	  shift, and coface-commutation compatibilities all hold with the prepend map acting as the
7704	  identity. Packaging \(h\) as a homotopy of cochain complexes gives \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet}
7705	  \simeq 0\).
7706	\end{proof}

7708	\begin{lemma}
7709	[Local vanishing of the evaluated augmented {\v C}ech homology]
7710	  \label{lem:cechSection_isZero_homology}
7711	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechSection_isZero_homology}
7712	  \uses{lem:cechSection_complex_iso, lem:cechSection_contractible,
7713	    lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7714	  Let \(V \le \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i\) for some index \(i\). Then for every
7715	  degree \(p\) the homology \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) of the augmented {\v C}ech complex evaluated at
7716	  \(V\) (the complex \(D^\bullet\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso}) is a zero
7717	  object; equivalently \(D^\bullet\) carries a contracting homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}
7718	  \simeq 0\). This is the residual obligation of Step~3 of
7719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}.
7720	\end{lemma}
7721	\begin{proof}
7722	  \uses{lem:cechSection_complex_iso, lem:cechSection_contractible,
7723	    lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7724	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso} the evaluated complex \(D^\bullet\) is
7725	  isomorphic to the concrete section {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\). The hypothesis \(V \le
7726	  \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i\) places \(V\) inside one cover member, so
7727	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_contractible} gives a contracting homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet}
7728	  \simeq 0\); transporting it across the complex isomorphism yields \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}
7729	  \simeq 0\). Lemma~\ref{lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero} then makes
7730	  \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) a zero object in every degree.
7731	\end{proof}

7800	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ

7805	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
7806	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
7807	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
7808	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
7809	  \[
7810	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
7811	      \;\cong\;
7812	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
7813	  \]
7814	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
7815	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
7816	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
7817	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
7818	\end{lemma}
7819	
7820	\begin{proof}
7821	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
7822	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
7823	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
7824	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
7825	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
7826	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
7827	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
7828	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
7829	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
7830	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
7831	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
7832	  additive homology-preserving functor.
7833	\end{proof}

7835	\begin{lemma}
7836	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
7837	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}

7839	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardResolutionPresheafComplex}
7840	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
7841	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
7842	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
7843	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
7844	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
7845	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
7846	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
7847	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
7848	  % associated to the presheaves
7849	  % $$
7850	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
7851	  % $$
7852	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
7853	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
7854	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
7855	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
7856	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
7857	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
7858	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
7859	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
7860	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
7861	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
7862	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
7863	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
7864	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
7865	  \[
7866	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
7867	      \;\cong\;
7868	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
7869	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
7870	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
7871	  \]
7872	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
7873	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
7874	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream
7875	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} and
7876	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
7877	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
7878	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
7879	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
7880	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
7881	
7882	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
7883	  of the preimage,
7884	  \[
7885	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
7886	      \;=\;
7887	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
7888	  \]
7889	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
7890	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the
7891	  resolution-internal sheafify-of-objectwise-homology form.
7892	\end{lemma}
7893	
7894	\begin{proof}
7895	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
7896	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
7897	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
7898	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
7899	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
7900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
7901	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
7902	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
7903	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
7904	  \[
7905	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
7906	      \;\cong\;
7907	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
7908	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
7909	  \]
7910	  which is the stated isomorphism. Since the sheafification of a presheaf vanishes
7911	  exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens, this yields the
7912	  affine-local vanishing criterion.
7913	\end{proof}

7952	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct

7954	  % L180--199).
7955	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
7956	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.

7958	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7959	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
7960	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
7961	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
7962	  \to S\). Then:

7964	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
7965	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
7966	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
7967	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
7968	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
7969	      \(k \geq 0\),
7970	      \[
7971	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
7972	      \]
7973	  \end{enumerate}
