7976	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
7977	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
7978	% the sheaf
7979	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
7980	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
7981	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
7982	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
7983	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
7984	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
7985	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
7986	% opens we win." (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L187--198)
7987	\begin{proof}
7988	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero, lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_locally_isZero, lem:ext_homcomplex_mathlib, lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_sections_locally_zero, lem:pushforward_sections_functor}
7989	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
7990	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
7991	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
7992	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
7993	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
7994	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
7995	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
7996	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
7997	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
7998	
7999	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
8000	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
8001	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
8002	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
8003	
8004	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
8005	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
8006	  sheafification of
8007	  \[
8008	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
8009	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
8010	  \]
8011	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
8012	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
8013	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
8014	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
8015	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
8016	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
8017	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
8018	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
8019	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
8020	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
8021	
8022	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
8023	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
8024	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
8025	  for every \(k\),
8026	  \[
8027	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
8028	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
8029	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
8030	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
8031	  \]
8032	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
8033	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
8034	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
8035	
8036	  \emph{Proof details.} The argument rests on three identifications.
8037	
8038	  \emph{(1) The cohomology-presheaf identification.} The objectwise homology in degree
8039	  \(n\) of the pushed-forward resolution complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\), evaluated at an open
8040	  \(V\), is identified with the absolute cohomology presheaf
8041	  \(V \mapsto H^n(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}) = \operatorname{Ext}^n\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8042	  \mathcal{G}\bigr)\). Concretely: \(\operatorname{Ext}^n(X, Y)\) over an injective resolution of
8043	  the second argument \(Y\) is identified with the \(n\)-th cohomology of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)
8044	  cochain complex \(H^n\bigl(\operatorname{Hom}(X[0], \mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)\), via the composite
8045	  of the Ext-to-cohomology-class equivalence and the cohomology-class-to-homology equivalence of the
8046	  Hom complex (the equivalences of Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homcomplex_mathlib}). Taking \(X = j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)}\) and \(Y =
8047	  \mathcal{G}\), the degree-\(k\) Hom group \(\operatorname{Hom}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8048	  \mathcal{I}^k)\) is identified with \(\Gamma(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{I}^k) = (f_*\mathcal{I}^k)(V)\) by
8049	  the corepresentability \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \dashv \Gamma(U, -)\) of the absolute-cohomology setup,
8050	  degreewise and naturally; the \(\mathbb{N}\)/\(\mathbb{Z}\) indexing of the two cochain complexes is
8051	  matched by the resolution's degreewise comparison isomorphism. The right-derived side is settled by
8052	  the already-used identification of \(R^n f_*\) with the homology of \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\)
8053	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}); no separate ``right-derived equals Ext'' comparison
8054	  lemma is needed, since both compute the same homology over the same injective resolution and meet at
8055	  corepresentability.
8056	
8057	  \emph{(2) Serre vanishing for a general affine open.} The Serre vanishing of
8058	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is stated on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\). For a general affine
8059	  open \(V\), the preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) (respectively \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\)) is again affine: \(j\) is
8060	  an affine morphism (the helper that \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is affine for \(U\) affine and \(X\)
8061	  separated), so preimages of affines under \(j\) are affine, and one transports the vanishing along
8062	  the canonical isomorphism \(j^{-1}(V) \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{O})\) using
8063	  the naturality of absolute cohomology under that isomorphism.
8064	
8065	  \emph{(3) Locally zero implies zero sheafification.} The
8066	  passage from ``\(H^k(\cdots) = 0\) on a basis of affine opens'' to ``\(R^k f_* = 0\)'' is the
8067	  module-sheafification analogue of Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero} (and of its packaged
8068	  form Lemma~\ref{lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_locally_isZero}). It is obtained by transporting the
8069	  abelian-sheaf statement across the natural isomorphism
8070	  \(\operatorname{toSheaf} \circ \operatorname{sheafify} \cong \operatorname{presheafToSheaf} \circ
8071	  \operatorname{forget}\) between module-sheafification followed by the forgetful functor and abelian
8072	  sheafification; the ``locally zero'' hypothesis it consumes is exactly the basis-of-affines vanishing
8073	  supplied by identifications (1) and~(2) above.
8074	\end{proof}
8075	
8076	\begin{lemma}
8077	[The pushforward-sections functor and its additivity]
8078	  \label{lem:pushforward_sections_functor}
8079	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSectionsFunctor,
8080	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSectionsFunctor_additive}
8081	  \uses{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
8082	  Let \(j : U \to X\) be a morphism and \(W \subseteq X\) an open. The
8083	  \emph{pushforward-sections functor} \(\operatorname{pushforwardSectionsFunctor} j\,W :
8084	  U.\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Ab}\) sends an \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module \(M\) to its sections
8085	  \(\Gamma(W, j_* M) = \Gamma(j^{-1}(W), M)\) --- the pushforward along \(j\), forgotten to
8086	  abelian groups, viewed as a presheaf and evaluated at \(W\). It is an additive functor, and
8087	  its \(q\)-th right derived functor applied to \(H\) computes the absolute cohomology
8088	  \(H^q(j^{-1}(W), H)\).
8089	\end{lemma}
8090	\begin{proof}
8091	  \uses{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
8092	  The functor is the composite of pushforward \(\operatorname{pushforward} j\) (additive), the
8093	  forgetful functor to presheaves of abelian groups (additive), and evaluation at \(W\)
8094	  (additive). A composite of additive functors is additive; the additivity follows from the additivity of
8095	  each factor. The identification of its right derived functor with
8096	  absolute cohomology is the presheaf description of
8097	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}.
8098	\end{proof}
8099

8099	
8100	\begin{lemma}
8101	[Sheafification of a sectionwise-locally-zero presheaf vanishes]
8102	  \label{lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_sections_locally_zero}
8103	  \lean{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_sections_locally_zero}
8104	  \uses{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero}
8105	  Let \(J\) be a Grothendieck topology on a site \(\mathcal{C}\) and \(Q\) a presheaf of
8106	  abelian groups. Suppose that for every object \(U\) and every section \(s \in Q(U)\) there
8107	  is a covering sieve \(S \in J(U)\) on which \(s\) restricts to \(0\) --- i.e.\ \(Q(g)(s) =
8108	  0\) for every arrow \(g : V \to U\) in \(S\). Then the sheafification of \(Q\) is the zero
8109	  sheaf.
8110	
8111	  This is the \emph{sectionwise} (local-injectivity) strengthening of
8112	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_locally_isZero}. That lemma requires the
8113	  \emph{objectwise} vanishing \(Q(V) = 0\) for every member \(V\) of a single covering sieve,
8114	  which forces the vanishing to hold on a downward-closed family. The present hypothesis only
8115	  asks each individual section to die on \emph{some} covering sieve, so the witnessing sieve
8116	  may be generated by a cofinal \emph{basis} of good opens (such as the affine opens, where
8117	  the relevant section group already vanishes) even though that basis is not downward closed
8118	  --- exactly the situation of the relative-affine vanishing argument, where the affine opens
8119	  are not downward closed in the opens topology.
8120	\end{lemma}
8121	\begin{proof}
8122	  \uses{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero}
8123	  Compare \(Q\) with the constant zero presheaf \(Z\) via the zero map \(0 : Q \to Z\). The
8124	  hypothesis, applied to a difference of sections \(x - y\), shows \(0\) is locally injective;
8125	  local surjectivity is automatic because the target is objectwise a zero object. Hence \(0 :
8126	  Q \to Z\) is locally bijective, i.e.\ lies in the local-isomorphism class \(J.W\), and
8127	  Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero} collapses the sheafification of \(Q\) to the
8128	  zero sheaf.
8129	\end{proof}
8130	
8131	\begin{lemma}
8132	[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
8133	  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
8134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
8135	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
8136	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
8137	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
8138	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
8139	  % L180--199).
8140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
8141	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
8142	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
8143	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
8144	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
8145	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
8146	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
8147	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
8148	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
8149	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
8150	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
8151	  \[
8152	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
8153	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
8154	  \]
8155	\end{lemma}
8156	
8157	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
8158	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
8159	% the sheaf
8160	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
8161	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
8162	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
8163	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
8164	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
8165	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
8166	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
8167	% opens we win."
8168	\begin{proof}
8169	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
8170	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
8171	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
8172	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
8173	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
8174	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
8175	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
8176	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
8177	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
8178	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
8179	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
8180	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
8181	
8182	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
8183	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
8184	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
8185	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
8186	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
8187	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
8188	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
8189	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
8190	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
8191	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
8192	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
8193	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
8194	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
8195	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
8196	  \(k \geq 1\).
8197	\end{proof}
8198	
8199	\begin{lemma}
8200	
8201	\leanok
8202	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
8203	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
8204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
8205	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
8206	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
8207	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
8208	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
8209	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
8210	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
8211	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
8212	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
8213	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
8214	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
8215	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
8216	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
8217	  % $$
8218	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
8219	  % $$
8220	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
8221	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
8222	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
8223	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
8224	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
8225	  % we have
8226	  % $$
8227	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
8228	  % $$
8229	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
8230	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
8231	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
8232	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
8233	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
8234	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
8235	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
8236	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
8237	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
8238	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
8239	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
8240	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
8241	  \[
8242	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
8243	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
8244	  \]
8245	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
8246	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
8247	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
8248	
8249	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
8250	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
8251	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
8252	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
8253	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
8254	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
8255	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
8256	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
8257	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
8258	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
8259	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
8260	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
8261	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
8262	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
8263	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
8264	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
8265	  role in the applications.
8266	\end{lemma}
8267	
8268	\begin{proof}
8269	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
8270	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
8271	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
8272	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
8273	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
8274	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
8275	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
8276	  \begin{enumerate}
8277	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
8278	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
8279	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
8280	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
8281	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
8282	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
8283	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
8284	  \end{enumerate}
8285	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
8286	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
8287	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
8288	  resolution,
8289	  \[
8290	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
8291	      \;\cong\;
8292	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
8293	  \]
8294	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
8295	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
8296	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
8297	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
8298	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
8299	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
8300	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
8301	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
8302	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
8303	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
8304	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
8305	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
8306	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
8307	\end{proof}

## Quality note: `lem:cehSection_contractible` and the dependent-engine pin

**Finding:** `lem:cechSection_contractible` carries `\uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}`.
The `CombinatorialCech.Dependent` engine (`depDiff`, `depHomotopy`, `depHomotopy_spec`,
`depDiff_exact`) has no standalone blueprint block; it lives under the
`lem:cech_acyclic_affine` node as infrastructure.

**Circularity check (passed):** The proof sketch for `lem:cechSection_contractible` reads
verbatim (paraphrase from the chapter):

> "Because `V ≤ coverOpen 𝒰 i_fix`, the restricted cover family `U'_j = U_j ∩ V` has a
> maximum: `U'_{i_fix} = V`, and every `U'_j ≤ V`. The prepend map `prepend i_fix` is
> therefore the identity on each coefficient `Γ(U'_σ, F)`. The dependent-coefficient
> combinatorial Čech engine supplies the contracting homotopy `h` with `d∘h + h∘d = id`."

No affine cohomology is invoked in this proof. The contractibility is purely
combinatorial — it depends only on the cone-point homotopy construction over
`{V ≤ coverOpen 𝒰 i_fix}`, which makes the prepend an identity. The `\uses` pin
on `lem:cech_acyclic_affine` is a **Lean infrastructure dependency** (the
`depDiff`/`depHomotopy` declarations are defined in that node's scope), not a
mathematical dependency on affine vanishing.

**Recommendation for the prover:** The `\uses` edge is accurate as a *Lean* dependency.
However, to avoid confusion about the mathematical content, the prover should mentally
read the pin as "import `CombinatorialCech.Dependent` engine" — not as "invoke affine
Čech vanishing." No blueprint change is needed; this is a reading note only.

**Severity:** Quality note. Does not block formalization.
